membership_details.description_of_your_organisation_or_if_a:
Edinburgh Youth Futures (EYF) is a medium-sized, voluntary sector organisation based in central Edinburgh, working primarily with children and young people aged 8–18 (and up to 25 for those with additional support needs) across the city and surrounding areas. We were established in 2009 by a group of local parents, teachers and youth workers who were concerned about the lack of coordinated, youth-led support for young people transitioning from upper primary into secondary school, and then on into further education, training or employment.

Our core mission is to ensure that all children and young people – particularly those from low‑income families, care-experienced young people, and those with additional support needs – have a genuine voice in decisions that affect them and access to the opportunities, relationships and resources they need to thrive. We deliver a mix of after‑school clubs, youth voice forums, mentoring, and targeted 1‑to‑1 support, with a strong emphasis on co‑production: young people sit on our Youth Advisory Board, help recruit staff, design programmes, and evaluate our work.

Current programmes include:
- **Bridging Steps**: a transition programme working with P7 and S1 pupils in four partner schools in North and West Edinburgh, focusing on confidence-building, peer connection, and early identification of support needs.
- **Young Voices Edinburgh**: a youth participation and advocacy project where 40–50 young people each year work on campaigns around mental health in schools, digital safety, and inclusive play spaces. Recently, a group of young people we support presented to the local authority’s Children and Families Committee about the impact of exam-related anxiety and were involved in shaping their school’s wellbeing strategy.
- **Mentor Connect**: a volunteer mentoring scheme that pairs trained adult mentors with young people aged 13–18 who are at risk of disengagement from education or who are struggling with confidence, social isolation, or caring responsibilities at home.
- **Inclusion in Action**: targeted group work for young people with additional support needs, including autistic young people and those with ADHD, focusing on social skills, self-advocacy, and transitions planning.

Our annual income currently sits in the £350,000–£400,000 range, coming from a mix of local authority contracts, trust and foundation grants, and individual donations. We employ 11 staff (7 FTE) and around 45 active volunteers. We work closely with local schools, social work teams, CAMHS, and other third sector partners.

We are applying to join Children in Scotland because we want to:
- Stay up to date with national policy developments around GIRFEC, The Promise, mental health, and education reform so we can better support our young people and ensure our practice is aligned with current guidance.
- Connect with other organisations across Scotland for peer learning, joint funding bids, and sharing of resources and tools (for example, evaluation frameworks or youth participation models).
- Access high-quality training for our staff and volunteers in areas like child protection, trauma‑informed practice, rights-based approaches, and meaningful youth participation.
- Create more opportunities for the young people we support to engage in national participation initiatives, consultations and advisory groups, so that their lived experience can directly inform policy and practice.

On a personal note, I (Fiona) have been working in youth and community work for over 12 years, starting as a youth worker in Wester Hailes and then moving into programme development and management roles. My particular interests are youth participation, mental health and wellbeing, and supporting smoother transitions between primary and secondary school. I’m also a parent of two children in local primary schools, and I’m very conscious of the pressures that children and families are facing – from the cost of living crisis to the ongoing impact of the pandemic on mental health and learning.

By joining Children in Scotland, I hope we can both contribute our learning – for example, around co‑produced youth voice projects and multi‑agency working – and learn from others who are further ahead in areas like rights-based practice, inclusive education, and anti-poverty work. We see membership as a way to strengthen our organisation, deepen our impact, and make sure that the voices of the young people we work with in Edinburgh are heard at a national level.
membership_details.where_did_you_hear_about_children_in_sco:
I first became aware of Children in Scotland several years ago when a colleague forwarded a link to one of your online conferences on children’s rights and participation. More recently, I have:
- Attended two of your online training sessions (one on trauma‑informed practice in schools and another on involving children and young people in strategic decision‑making).
- Seen regular updates and policy briefings shared by partner organisations such as Children 1st and Young Scot via Twitter/X and LinkedIn.
- Heard positive feedback from a fellow programme manager at a neighbouring youth organisation (Leith Youth Hub), who mentioned that membership has been really valuable for networking and staying on top of policy changes.
- Come across your resources while searching for materials on The Promise and the incorporation of the UNCRC in Scotland.

After hearing about Children in Scotland through these various channels and noticing how often other respected organisations referenced your work, it felt like a natural step for Edinburgh Youth Futures to become a member so we can engage more systematically with your networks, events and resources.
